For separable Hamiltonians H(q,p) = T(p) + V(q), where T is kinetic energy and V is potential energy, these methods simplify considerably.

Example 8.1 (Harmonic Oscillator). Consider the Hamiltonian H(q,p) = ½p² + ½q². The symplectic Euler method (implicit form) gives: - qₙ₊₁ = qₙ + h pₙ - pₙ₊₁ = pₙ - h qₙ₊₁

This can be written as the linear map: \[\begin{pmatrix} qₙ₊₁ \\ pₙ₊₁ \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & h \\ -h & 1-h² \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} qₙ \\ pₙ \end{pmatrix}\]

One can verify that this matrix is symplectic: M^T J M = J.

using LinearAlgebra, Plots

# Symplectic Euler for harmonic oscillator
function symplectic_euler_harmonic(q0, p0, h, N)
    q, p = zeros(N+1), zeros(N+1)
    q[1], p[1] = q0, p0
    
    for n in 1:N
        q[n+1] = q[n] + h * p[n]
        p[n+1] = p[n] - h * q[n+1]
    end
    
    return q, p
end

# Compare with standard Euler (non-symplectic)
function standard_euler_harmonic(q0, p0, h, N)
    q, p = zeros(N+1), zeros(N+1)
    q[1], p[1] = q0, p0
    
    for n in 1:N
        q[n+1] = q[n] + h * p[n]
        p[n+1] = p[n] - h * q[n]  # Uses q[n] instead of q[n+1]
    end
    
    return q, p
end

# Simulation parameters
q0, p0 = 1.0, 0.0
h = 0.1
N = 200

# Run simulations
q_symp, p_symp = symplectic_euler_harmonic(q0, p0, h, N)
q_std, p_std = standard_euler_harmonic(q0, p0, h, N)

# Energy comparison
H_symp = 0.5 * (q_symp.^2 + p_symp.^2)  # Discrete energy
H_std = 0.5 * (q_std.^2 + p_std.^2)

println("Symplectic Euler energy drift: ", H_symp[end] - H_symp[1])
println("Standard Euler energy drift: ", H_std[end] - H_std[1])

# Phase space plots show symplectic method maintains bounded orbits
# while standard Euler exhibits artificial damping or growth

5.2.2 8.2.2 Störmer-Verlet Method

The Störmer-Verlet algorithm is the most widely used symplectic integrator in molecular dynamics and celestial mechanics.

Definition 8.4 (Störmer-Verlet Method). For a separable Hamiltonian H(q,p) = T(p) + V(q), the Störmer-Verlet method is: 1. pₙ₊₁/₂ = pₙ - (h/2) ∇V(qₙ) 2. qₙ₊₁ = qₙ + h ∇T(pₙ₊₁/₂) 3. pₙ₊₁ = pₙ₊₁/₂ - (h/2) ∇V(qₙ₊₁)

For the common case T(p) = ½p²/m, this simplifies to the position form: \[qₙ₊₁ = 2qₙ - qₙ₋₁ + h²m⁻¹ F(qₙ)\] where F = -∇V is the force.

Theorem 8.2. The Störmer-Verlet method is symplectic and second-order accurate.

Proof sketch. The method can be viewed as the composition of three symplectic maps, each corresponding to evolution under T or V alone. Since the composition of symplectic transformations is symplectic, the full method preserves symplectic structure. Second-order accuracy follows from the symmetric splitting of the Hamiltonian. □

Example 8.2 (N-body Gravitational System). Consider N particles with masses mᵢ and positions qᵢ ∈ R³, interacting via Newtonian gravity:

\[H(q,p) = \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{|pᵢ|²}{2mᵢ} - \sum_{i<j} \frac{G mᵢ mⱼ}{|qᵢ - qⱼ|}\]

using StaticArrays

struct NBodySystem
    masses::Vector{Float64}
    G::Float64
end

function gravitational_force!(F, q, system)
    N = length(system.masses)
    fill!(F, 0.0)
    
    for i in 1:N
        for j in i+1:N
            r_vec = q[j] - q[i]
            r_mag = norm(r_vec)
            r_hat = r_vec / r_mag
            
            # Force magnitude
            F_mag = system.G * system.masses[i] * system.masses[j] / r_mag^2
            F_vec = F_mag * r_hat
            
            F[i] += F_vec
            F[j] -= F_vec
        end
    end
end

function verlet_nbody!(q, p, system, dt)
    N = length(system.masses)
    F = [zero(SVector{3, Float64}) for _ in 1:N]
    
    # Kick: p_{n+1/2} = p_n - (dt/2) * ∇V(q_n)
    gravitational_force!(F, q, system)
    for i in 1:N
        p[i] += (dt/2) * F[i]
    end
    
    # Drift: q_{n+1} = q_n + dt * p_{n+1/2} / m
    for i in 1:N
        q[i] += dt * p[i] / system.masses[i]
    end
    
    # Kick: p_{n+1} = p_{n+1/2} - (dt/2) * ∇V(q_{n+1})
    gravitational_force!(F, q, system)
    for i in 1:N
        p[i] += (dt/2) * F[i]
    end
end

# Example: Earth-Sun system
earth_sun = NBodySystem([5.972e24, 1.989e30], 6.674e-11)  # kg, m³/kg/s²

# Initialize circular orbit (requires careful initial conditions)
q = [SVector{3}([1.496e11, 0.0, 0.0]),    # Earth position (1 AU)
     SVector{3}([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])]          # Sun at origin

p = [SVector{3}([0.0, 2.98e4 * 5.972e24, 0.0]),  # Earth momentum
     SVector{3}([0.0, -2.98e4 * 5.972e24, 0.0])]  # Conservation of momentum

# Integrate for one year
dt = 3600.0  # 1 hour timestep
steps_per_year = Int(365 * 24)

for step in 1:steps_per_year
    verlet_nbody!(q, p, earth_sun, dt)
end

println("Final Earth position: ", q[1])
println("Orbital period preserved: ", norm(q[1]) ≈ 1.496e11)

5.2.3 8.2.3 Gauss-Legendre Runge-Kutta Methods

Higher-order symplectic integrators can be constructed using implicit Runge-Kutta methods based on Gauss-Legendre quadrature.

Definition 8.5 (Gauss-Legendre RK Method). The s-stage Gauss-Legendre Runge-Kutta method has order 2s and is defined by the Butcher tableau with coefficients aᵢⱼ and bᵢ derived from Gauss-Legendre quadrature nodes and weights.

Theorem 8.3. Gauss-Legendre Runge-Kutta methods applied to Hamiltonian systems are symplectic.

The 2-stage Gauss-Legendre method (order 4) has nodes c₁ = (3-√3)/6, c₂ = (3+√3)/6 and is particularly popular for long-time integrations requiring high accuracy.

5.3 8.3 Long-Time Energy Behavior

While symplectic integrators do not preserve the Hamiltonian exactly, they exhibit remarkable long-time stability properties.

Theorem 8.4 (Backward Error Analysis). A symplectic method applied to a Hamiltonian system conserves exactly a nearby Hamiltonian H̃ = H + hᵖEₚ + O(hᵖ⁺¹), where p is the order of the method.

This backward error analysis, pioneered by Hairer and Lubich, explains why symplectic methods maintain energy oscillations bounded over exponentially long times, rather than exhibiting systematic drift.

Example 8.3 (Long-term Energy Conservation). Consider the Hénon-Heiles system: \[H(q₁,q₂,p₁,p₂) = \frac{1}{2}(p₁² + p₂²) + \frac{1}{2}(q₁² + q₂²) + q₁²q₂ - \frac{1}{3}q₂³\]

using DifferentialEquations

function henon_heiles!(du, u, p, t)
    q1, q2, p1, p2 = u
    
    # Hamilton's equations
    du[1] = p1  # dq1/dt
    du[2] = p2  # dq2/dt
    du[3] = -q1 - 2*q1*q2  # dp1/dt
    du[4] = -q2 - q1^2 + q2^2  # dp2/dt
end

function henon_heiles_energy(u)
    q1, q2, p1, p2 = u
    return 0.5*(p1^2 + p2^2) + 0.5*(q1^2 + q2^2) + q1^2*q2 - q2^3/3
end

# Initial conditions for chaotic trajectory
u0 = [0.0, 0.1, 0.5, 0.0]
tspan = (0.0, 1000.0)  # Very long integration time

# Compare Verlet (symplectic) with Tsit5 (non-symplectic)
prob_verlet = ODEProblem(henon_heiles!, u0, tspan)
sol_verlet = solve(prob_verlet, VelocityVerlet(), dt=0.01, adaptive=false)

prob_tsit = ODEProblem(henon_heiles!, u0, tspan)
sol_tsit = solve(prob_tsit, Tsit5(), abstol=1e-12, reltol=1e-12)

# Energy evolution
times = 0:1:1000
energy_verlet = [henon_heiles_energy(sol_verlet(t)) for t in times]
energy_tsit = [henon_heiles_energy(sol_tsit(t)) for t in times]

println("Verlet energy variation: ", maximum(energy_verlet) - minimum(energy_verlet))
println("Tsit5 energy drift: ", energy_tsit[end] - energy_tsit[1])

# [FIGURE: Energy vs time showing bounded oscillations for Verlet, 
#  systematic drift for Tsit5 despite smaller local errors]

5.4 8.4 Lie Group Integrators

Many differential equations evolve on manifolds with Lie group structure, such as rotations SO(3) or special linear groups. Standard integrators can cause the solution to drift off the manifold, while Lie group methods preserve the geometric structure exactly.

5.4.1 8.4.1 Exponential Integration on Lie Groups

Definition 8.6 (Lie Group Integrator). For a differential equation ẏ = f(y) where y(t) ∈ G is a Lie group element, the exponential Lie group integrator is: yₙ₊₁ = yₙ exp(h A(yₙ)) where A(y) ∈ g is the corresponding element in the Lie algebra g, and exp: g → G is the exponential map.

Example 8.4 (Rigid Body Rotation). The rotation of a rigid body under no external torque follows: \[\dot{R} = R[\omega]×\] where R ∈ SO(3) is the rotation matrix and [ω]× is the skew-symmetric matrix representing angular velocity ω.

using LinearAlgebra

# Skew-symmetric matrix from vector
function skew(ω)
    return [0 -ω[3] ω[2]; ω[3] 0 -ω[1]; -ω[2] ω[1] 0]
end

# Matrix exponential for SO(3) using Rodrigues' formula
function so3_exp(ω)
    θ = norm(ω)
    if θ < 1e-12
        return I + skew(ω) + 0.5*skew(ω)^2
    else
        ω_hat = ω / θ
        return I + sin(θ)*skew(ω_hat) + (1-cos(θ))*skew(ω_hat)^2
    end
end

function rigid_body_step!(R, ω, dt)
    # For free rigid body: ω = constant (Euler's equations)
    # More generally, ω would be updated according to Euler's equations
    R[:,:] = R * so3_exp(dt * ω)
    
    # Explicitly orthogonalize to combat numerical drift
    # (Not needed for exact exponential, but good practice)
    U, _, V = svd(R)
    R[:,:] = U * V'
end

# Simulation
R = Matrix{Float64}(I, 3, 3)  # Initial rotation
ω = [0.1, 0.2, 0.05]  # Angular velocity (rad/s)
dt = 0.01
steps = 1000

# Track orthogonality (should remain 1.0)
orthogonality = Float64[]

for step in 1:steps
    rigid_body_step!(R, ω, dt)
    push!(orthogonality, det(R))  # Should be +1 for proper rotation
end

println("Final determinant (should be 1.0): ", det(R))
println("Orthogonality maintained: ", all(abs.(orthogonality .- 1.0) .< 1e-12))

# Angular momentum conservation (L = J*ω for diagonal inertia tensor J)
J = diagm([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])  # Principal moments of inertia
L_initial = J * ω
# For free rigid body, angular momentum in space frame is conserved
L_space = R * L_initial  # Should equal J * R * ω
println("Angular momentum conserved: ", norm(L_space - J * (R * ω)) < 1e-10)

5.4.2 8.4.2 Magnus Integrators

For linear matrix differential equations Ẏ = A(t)Y with Y(t) ∈ GL(n), Magnus integrators use the Magnus expansion to construct high-order exponential methods.

Definition 8.7 (Magnus Integrator). The Magnus expansion represents the solution as Y(t) = Y₀ exp(Ω(t)), where Ω(t) satisfies: \[Ω(t) = \int_0^t A(s) ds + \frac{1}{2}\int_0^t \int_0^s [A(τ), A(s)] dτ ds + \ldots\]

The fourth-order Magnus integrator uses: \[Ωₙ = h A((tₙ + tₙ₊₁)/2) + \frac{h²}{12}[A(tₙ), A(tₙ₊₁)]\]

5.5 8.5 Discrete Exterior Calculus

For partial differential equations with geometric structure, discrete exterior calculus provides a framework for structure-preserving finite element methods.

5.5.1 8.5.1 Discrete Differential Forms

Definition 8.8 (Simplicial Complex). A simplicial complex K is a finite collection of simplices such that: (1) every face of a simplex in K is also in K, and (2) the intersection of any two simplices in K is either empty or a common face.

Discrete differential forms are defined on the simplices of different dimensions: - 0-forms (scalars) live on vertices - 1-forms (vectors) live on edges
- 2-forms (area elements) live on faces - 3-forms (volume elements) live on tetrahedra

The discrete exterior derivative d maps k-forms to (k+1)-forms and satisfies d∘d = 0, preserving the fundamental identity from smooth exterior calculus.

5.5.2 8.5.2 Compatible Discretizations

Definition 8.9 (Compatible Discretization). A discretization of a differential equation is compatible if it preserves the discrete analogs of all geometric structures (exact sequences, Stokes’ theorem, conservation laws) present in the continuous problem.

Example 8.5 (Maxwell’s Equations). The compatible discretization of Maxwell’s equations places: - Electric field E on edges (1-forms) - Magnetic field B on faces (2-forms)
- Charge density ρ on vertices (0-forms) - Current density J on edges (1-forms)

using SparseArrays

struct MaxwellMesh
    vertices::Matrix{Float64}  # 3 × n_vertices
    edges::Matrix{Int}         # 2 × n_edges (vertex indices)
    faces::Matrix{Int}         # 3 × n_faces (vertex indices)
    
    # Discrete differential operators
    grad::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int}  # 0-forms → 1-forms
    curl::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int}  # 1-forms → 2-forms
    div::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int}   # 2-forms → 3-forms
    
    # Hodge stars (metric-dependent)
    hodge1::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int}  # 1-forms → 2-forms
    hodge2::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int}  # 2-forms → 1-forms
end

function build_grad_matrix(vertices, edges)
    n_vertices, n_edges = size(vertices, 2), size(edges, 2)
    I_vals, J_vals, V_vals = Int[], Int[], Float64[]
    
    for e in 1:n_edges
        v1, v2 = edges[1,e], edges[2,e]
        # Discrete gradient: +1 at tail, -1 at head
        push!(I_vals, e); push!(J_vals, v1); push!(V_vals, -1.0)
        push!(I_vals, e); push!(J_vals, v2); push!(V_vals, +1.0)
    end
    
    return sparse(I_vals, J_vals, V_vals, n_edges, n_vertices)
end

function build_curl_matrix(edges, faces)
    # Implementation depends on orientation of faces...
    # Returns matrix mapping edge-based 1-forms to face-based 2-forms
    # Satisfies curl∘grad = 0 by construction
end

function maxwell_timestep!(E, B, mesh, dt, ε₀=1.0, μ₀=1.0)
    # Leapfrog scheme preserving Gauss's laws
    
    # ∂B/∂t = -curl(E)
    curl_E = mesh.curl * E
    B .-= dt * curl_E
    
    # ∂E/∂t = (1/μ₀ε₀) curl(B) - J/ε₀
    # Using discrete Hodge star to convert between 1-forms and 2-forms
    curl_B = mesh.hodge2 * (mesh.curl' * (mesh.hodge1 * B))
    E .+= dt / (μ₀ * ε₀) * curl_B
    
    # Gauss's laws automatically satisfied if initially satisfied
    # due to discrete exactness: div∘curl = 0
end

5.6 8.6 Positivity-Preserving Schemes

Many physical systems require solutions to remain positive (concentrations, populations, probabilities). Standard high-order methods can produce negative values, leading to nonphysical behavior.

5.6.1 8.6.1 Strong Stability Preserving Methods

Definition 8.10 (Strong Stability Preserving). A method is strong stability preserving (SSP) with coefficient C if it can be written as a convex combination of forward Euler steps with step size Cₕ, where the forward Euler method preserves the desired property.

Example 8.6 (Population Dynamics with Migration). Consider the system: \[\frac{du}{dt} = Au + f(u)\] where u ≥ 0 represents population densities, A has non-positive diagonal and non-negative off-diagonal entries (migration matrix), and f(u) ≥ 0 when u ≥ 0.

function ssp_rk3_step!(u_new, u, A, f, dt)
    n = length(u)
    u1 = similar(u)
    u2 = similar(u)
    
    # Stage 1: u¹ = uⁿ + dt(Auⁿ + f(uⁿ))
    u1 .= u .+ dt * (A * u .+ f.(u))
    
    # Stage 2: u² = (3/4)uⁿ + (1/4)u¹ + (1/4)dt(Au¹ + f(u¹))
    u2 .= 0.75 * u .+ 0.25 * u1 .+ 0.25 * dt * (A * u1 .+ f.(u1))
    
    # Stage 3: uⁿ⁺¹ = (1/3)uⁿ + (2/3)u² + (2/3)dt(Au² + f(u²))
    u_new .= (1.0/3.0) * u .+ (2.0/3.0) * u2 .+ (2.0/3.0) * dt * (A * u2 .+ f.(u2))
    
    # Ensure positivity (projection step if needed)
    u_new .= max.(u_new, 0.0)
end

# Example: 3-species competition with migration
function competition_birth(u, r=1.0, K=10.0)
    return r * u * (1 - u/K)
end

# Migration matrix (species move between patches)
A = [-0.1  0.05  0.05;
      0.05 -0.1   0.05;
      0.05  0.05 -0.1]

u = [2.0, 3.0, 1.5]  # Initial populations
dt = 0.1
steps = 100

u_new = similar(u)
for step in 1:steps
    ssp_rk3_step!(u_new, u, A, competition_birth, dt)
    u, u_new = u_new, u
end

println("Final populations (all positive): ", u)
println("Positivity preserved: ", all(u .≥ 0))

5.6.2 8.6.2 Production-Destruction Form

Many reaction-diffusion systems can be written in production-destruction form, enabling specialized positive-preserving schemes.

Definition 8.11 (Production-Destruction Form). A system ∂u/∂t = P(u) - D(u)u is in production-destruction form if P(u) ≥ 0 and D(u) ≥ 0 for u ≥ 0.

The implicit scheme uₙ₊₁ = (uₙ + dt P(uₙ))/(1 + dt D(uₙ)) preserves positivity unconditionally.

5.7 8.7 Mass Conservation and Discrete Variational Methods

5.7.1 8.7.1 Finite Element Mass Lumping

For conservation laws, maintaining discrete mass conservation is crucial for long-term accuracy.

using Ferrite

function setup_mass_lumped_fem(grid)
    # Create consistent mass matrix
    M_consistent = create_sparsity_pattern(grid)
    
    # Assemble consistent mass matrix using standard FEM
    assembler = start_assemble(M_consistent)
    for cell in CellIterator(grid)
        # ... standard mass matrix assembly ...
    end
    M_consistent = end_assemble(assembler)
    
    # Lumping: row-sum -> diagonal
    M_lumped = spdiagm(vec(sum(M_consistent, dims=2)))
    
    return M_lumped
end

# Mass-conserving time stepping for heat equation
# ∂u/∂t = ∇·(k∇u) + f
function heat_step_mass_conservative!(u_new, u, M_lumped, K, f, dt)
    # Implicit Euler with lumped mass matrix
    # M_lumped * u_new = M_lumped * u + dt * (-K * u_new + M_lumped * f)
    # (M_lumped + dt * K) * u_new = M_lumped * (u + dt * f)
    
    rhs = M_lumped * (u .+ dt * f)
    lhs_matrix = M_lumped + dt * K
    
    u_new .= lhs_matrix \ rhs
    
    # Check mass conservation: ∫u dx should be conserved if ∫f dx = 0
    # This is exactly satisfied with lumped mass matrix
end

5.7.2 8.7.2 Variational Integrators

Variational integrators are derived from discrete versions of Hamilton’s principle, automatically preserving symplectic structure and momentum maps.

Definition 8.12 (Discrete Lagrangian). A discrete Lagrangian Lₐ(q₀,q₁,h) approximates the action integral ∫₀ʰ L(q,q̇) dt for trajectories connecting q₀ to q₁ in time h.

The discrete Euler-Lagrange equations are: \[D₂Lₐ(qₙ₋₁,qₙ,h) + D₁Lₐ(qₙ,qₙ₊₁,h) = 0\]

This defines an implicit map (qₙ₋₁,qₙ) ↦ (qₙ,qₙ₊₁) that is automatically symplectic.

5.8 8.8 Applications

5.8.1 8.8.1 Molecular
